procedure.requested_by: Dr. Paul Adams, MD — Spine Surgery

denial.reason_text: Physical therapy requirement not met. Plan received PT records from Valley Physical Therapy documenting 4 weeks of treatment. Member claims 8 total weeks but second provider (Mountain PT) records not received by plan. Cannot verify claimed treatment without complete records.

appeal_basis: Mountain PT records enclosed with this appeal — 4 additional weeks confirmed
Total 8 weeks PT completed across two providers
Records were submitted with initial request but may not have been received

mri_1.findings: C6-C7 large disc herniation, bilateral C7 nerve root compression, bilateral foraminal stenosis, mild cord contact without compression

neurological_exam.findings: Bilateral C7 dermatomal symptoms: bilateral tricep weakness 4/5, bilateral tricep reflex diminished, bilateral finger tingling

physical_therapy_provider_2.records_status: SUBMITTED WITH APPEAL — NOT RECEIVED AT INITIAL REVIEW
physical_therapy_provider_2.outcome_note: No improvement in neurological symptoms. Plateau documented by PT at discharge.

physical_therapy_summary.note: 8 weeks total PT across two providers. Both sets of records now submitted. Plateau documented at provider 2 discharge.

bilateral_c7_epidural.response: Left side: 50% temporary relief. Right side: 30% temporary relief. Both returned to baseline.

question: Should the prior authorization denial for ACDF C6-C7 (CPT 22551) be overturned on appeal?

ground_truth_complexity.obvious_reading: OVERTURN — additional PT records now submitted. 8 weeks total. All criteria met.
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_1: There was a 17-day gap in PT (June 28 to July 15) — does the gap break the continuity requirement for PT?
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_2: The plan denied because records were not received — if records were submitted but not received, is this a plan administrative failure or a member documentation failure?
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_3: Does the plan's failure to receive submitted records constitute a denial notice defect under CHSC 1374.31?
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_4: Is this a straightforward OVERTURN on the merits once the missing records are provided?
ground_truth_complexity.the_genuine_tension: With the Mountain PT records, all criteria are met. The 17-day gap between providers does not break the PT requirement — plan criteria specify duration, not continuity. The administrative failure (records not received) is the plan's problem to explain.
ground_truth_complexity.right_answer: OVERTURN — With Mountain PT records now submitted, member has completed 8 weeks PT, two pharmacotherapy agents, and interventional procedure. All criteria met. The 17-day gap between providers does not break the PT requirement. The initial denial was based on incomplete information due to record receipt failure.
